text: This meeting is being recorded.
How are you?
Good.
How are you?
It's nice to meet you.
You too.
Do you go by brand?
No.
It just shortened my name.
Brandon is good.
Okay.
I was like, all right, cool.
Well, nice to meet you.
Sorry I'm a little bit late.
No problem.
Excited to chat.
I can start by giving you a bit of a background on the role and how we're thinking about.
Got it.
And then from there, we can talk about you and I'll try and leave some time at the end for questions if that works for you.
Sounds perfect.
Perfect.
So this role, GrowthX, just to take a step back, we are a services agency.
So operate in some ways like a traditional marketing agency.
And the difference is that we're leveraging an internal platform that builds all of our content using AI workflows.
And so eventually that platform will become the product, but services, there's like a huge opportunity in services.
Like the TAM is much larger.
Budgets are totally different.
And I think like one of the advantages of GrowthX is human in the loop we're really doing.
Like we're taking a strategic approach and actually solving the problem for them because, you know, anybody that's tried.
Do this on their own, knows that AI is finicky, and you need to like pull from a lot of different sources, you can't just use one model, it won't work.
So there are a lot of problems with that.
So we kind of solve that problem, generate content, deliver for clients.

What we the way that the delivery or operates is we have like a pod model, we run those the AI workflows, we have managing editors, we have kind of like, high level account managers, account strategists, account directors.
But what we're finding is that all of those people get super close to the client, in terms of just like seeing the trees instead of the forest.
And so what we're looking for is somebody that can help us kind of on a quarterly basis, as the clients are rolling through, be a little bit of a floating, contribute, see, take a step back.
And, you know, we generate strategies.
That are like two to three months, like running two to three months.
And then we want to reevaluate.
We want to look at signals.
And this is, that's where this SEO strategist role comes in.

So we're hoping for somebody that has that experience, like can run the like data analytics, look a little bit at signals and help kind of redirect that strategy and think a little bit higher level as we are like changing.

The direction of deliverables on an ongoing basis so that we continue to provide like value and make sure that we're being objective in our delivery and not just like getting stuck in a rut, which I think can be very easy when you're working with a client day to day.
You just kind of like get sucked into what you're doing and what they're doing.
And so we're hoping to have starting with one person that can like take a step back and just help provide that account strategist or like that strategy.
And so
From an SEO and also from a Geo perspective.
Obviously, that's like top of mind for a lot of clients.
So some can help like run experiments and just keep tracking signals writ large so that we can continue to bring like best practices for especially for Geo as we're learning.
Does that make sense?
Absolutely.
Yeah, I love it.
Awesome.
Well, with that, I would love to like learn a little bit about you and your background.
Yeah, so jumping into my experience, when I was, I started basically my technology journey when I was younger.
I've always loved, you know, trying to help people find what they need to find.
During my master's program, I was really passionate about this.
And my thesis was the creation of an augmented reality app that turned into a marketing campaign that drove people to local businesses through kind of.
They scanned mural art, and then they would choose their own adventure and choose where to go in the city, which increased local foot traffic and improved their overall marketing and business.
So that was a really cool step into the world of not only how can technology be used for good, but also how can it be used to drive business and traffic as well.
From there, while I was going into that master's program, going through it, I also was doing social media and SEO auditing and implementation.
Basically, what that involved was making sure that content was met from a certain set of quality guidelines.

It was created with the best formats, and I would audit and improve this content from multiple platforms and multiple websites, kind of going through each one and giving the client suggestions on how they can improve their marketing overall.
So that was a good implementation and step into my next job, which was Embark Behavioral Health.
So they were looking for someone in SEO who also...
I wanted to be in the health space.
I'm really passionate about not only technology, but how it can be used to scale and grow and build something new that's forward-thinking and embark behavioral health.
I was really passionate about doing this in terms of fully building a startup marketing team that could scale and drive ROI.
When I started, there was nobody except for an editor and a marketing manager on the team.
And their traffic was around 5,000 people monthly or less.
And by the time I left, was over 130,000.
And their keyword growth had been more than 40,000 as keywords and 2,000 top positions.
So from the perspective of building a business from the ground up, I love getting to be a part of that team and trying to basically work with IT teams and clinical teams.
Compliance teams, web developers, lead generation specialists, and kind of jumping into show.
This is the best practice, not only for SEO now, but AEO and GEO as it evolved.
And that was kind of in the middle of my time at Embark that it really started scaling out.
And one of my biggest things that I really loved at Embark was creating a content engine that was AI-driven that basically scaled our traffic and keywords and ramped them up huge.
So I love that Growthx.ai is doing kind of what I'm passionate about, and that's building those growth engines that kind of drive that growth and really can build businesses up.
And that's something I'm really passionate about.
Awesome.
I mean, those numbers are really, really impressive.
What did you do?
Like, you know, what were the individual actions that you took and how did you think about strategy over three years?
Yeah, absolutely.
So during my position, I was pretty much, I was in charge of every aspect of SEO.
So this included, you know, obviously keyword research.
On-page brief creation, content creation, editing content, working with clinicians as well to create that content.
So sometimes geography work, as well as the technical aspect of it.

So doing technical audits for more than 15 client websites and managing different teams for each of those 15 teams for tone, brand voice, marketing goals, meetings throughout the week, and making sure that I created syndicated content strategies that were focused on their niche topical authority.
So that looked like doing all of those actions for them at 15 times.
So it was a lot of different things at the same time.
So like I mentioned, keyword creation, content brief, technical audits, as well as local SEO.
So a lot of my job was also creating and syndicating directories to make sure those were in compliance.
Also community building.
So working with communities, responding to queries, inquiries, and making...
Making sure that client questions were addressed, both on social media and directories, backlink building, so making sure that we were building topical niche authority in the space.

When I started, these websites were getting backlinks from a bunch of low-quality sources, and I made sure to syndicate that into topical authority niches, reaching out to key players, like VeryWellMind, VeryWellHealth, getting them on those names, and linking back to us.

And, as well as the content growth engine side of things, so I was really, really focused on content and growth when was there, and so I would interview the clinicians myself, take that, syndicate it using Zapier and AI tools like ScaleNet, Jasper, and Webhooks, and kind of spread that out and syndicate that content, creating a repeatable growth engine.
That wasn't just content out there, but in, it was very, your money, your life focused, and high quality.
So, every aspect of STL was part of, and managing, and it was definitely a lot of different things.
I loved kind of saying, here are the key things we can do to move this business and seeing that grow.
Yeah, yeah.
I mean, these results are really great over time.
How did you think about like short-term versus long-term and balancing the priorities, but making sure that you were going in the right direction?
Absolutely.
So in the marketing team, and I think just when you're building a business in general, they would come up to me a lot and say like, how about we do this thing?
How about we change the font or the styling?

And what I would say, and this is kind of how I approach power sprints, 30, 60, 90-day increments to make sure clients get result, is create a campaign strategy sprint that's focused on the biggest wins, the low-hanging fruit, and the technical and the content growth engines, programmatic growth, making sure that I focus on those things first.
So I would always focus on those technical and low-hanging fruit things first.
So on a micro scale, like just jumping into that meta title, meta description, on the key pages that are getting high traffic, but not high traffic.
Click-through rate, making sure we improve those.
Focusing on the key pages that are maybe missing the conversational queries that AEO and GEO engines are looking for and making sure and improve that so we're showing up.
So, and at the same time, working with clients to say, I understand you want to maybe change the font here or change this here.
And then kind of saying, this is our strategy.
We need to stick with this to make sure we get the best results, but we're going to put this on our list and this strategy is going to build the most for you.
So, I would always stick to that sprint plan as well as, you know, Kanban boards, project management tools to show them, this is what we're doing.
And this is how it relates to the metrics that you want to see as well.
Awesome.
Talk to me about a time when you had like changing client needs or, you know, the company was changing needs and you had to adjust quickly.
Yeah, absolutely.
Embark had a lot of service changes where they would say, one example of this is they.
We some treatment centers that were no longer operable.
And part of that is pushback from the industry.
There was a lot of changes in how people kind of perceived these treatment centers.
And so to combat that, we had to pivot really quickly and do full website kind of full campaign sweeps of redirection.
So we would take a full website and say, okay, we need to take this, redirect all the relevant pages to ones on a different site, consolidate authority, take down directories.
So the way I did this was break down a quick 30-day campaign with the highest impact things we could do first and make sure that key players knew what things were going to happen.
So, for example, taking down directories, there was a key date to make sure people knew this is when it's personally going to be viewable as the business going offline.
Here's where we're going to direct it to, not only the page, but the phone lines too.
I did a lot of that at Embark as well.
So making sure that the phone lines and Invoca were going to the right place.
So on a micro scale, I created redirect maps for the web developers, making sure that they knew exactly

We helped implement all of that, created public-first PR announcements, making sure people understood why we were closing, and that was dispersed and syndicated across all content mediums, and then redirected key pages at key points we identified in that 30-day sprint.
Yeah.
Okay, cool.
I mean, as you're setting things up, changing, how do you document your processes to make sure that others are aware and for others to be able to use going forward?
Absolutely.
Yeah.
At Monday, or excuse me, I like to use Monday for a lot of, for a lot of this stuff where we actually can share across multiple teams.
This is what we're doing, this is when it's happening, and this is how you can see.

So that each, each, like, IT team can see, or the compliance team can see, like, this is when we need your sign-off for this key document to be signed off on, or this is when we need the IT team to make sure that.
This form system is working appropriately.
So I set up key sprints, key input dates to make sure that stakeholders know, this is what we're doing, this is what needs to be done, and this is our publish date.
So that we not only get there or sign off, but we stay on schedule too.
Yeah.
And then how are you communicating your strategy and results to teams that might not be as technical or other non-technical clients or audiences?
Yeah, that's a huge thing, especially for SEO.
Sometimes I use the acronyms and people are like, their eyes glaze over.
And so I have to be really careful at Embark.
I did this a lot communicating with C-suite teams and clinical teams, making sure that what I'm communicating is in the language that they understand.
So for the C-suite and the financial team, would say, I wouldn't focus on leading indicators in those reports.
For example, keywords, rankings, backlinks, all that stuff.
I would focus on what they understood.
So CAC, lifetime value, return on investment.

Monetary metrics that really make sense to those key players, tying those into Looker Studio dashboards to make sure that they understand, okay, I don't need to know all the stuff that goes in behind it.
I just need to know, are we driving that revenue up for this key thing?
And how does it impact me?
And consolidating that into some key visuals to make sure they understand that.
So I like to make sure the story I'm telling is right for each person I'm telling it to.
Yeah.
What tools are you using for visuals and tracking?
Well, use Looker Studio a lot syndicated across multiple tools like SEMrush and Search Console to make sure that I'm pulling in that data.
I like to focus on non-vanity metrics when possible.
So like SEMrush keyword rankings, all that stuff is good.
But going straight to the source of Search Console or GA4 is also huge, especially I like to show the conversion value and make sure that the metrics in GA4 itself are tied to real world things.
So pulling from GA4 and Search Console are huge.

into Looker Studio, but some vanity metrics are nice from the AI visibility standpoint, especially for SEMrush with their tool, to show businesses, okay, like, this is what search engines may think of you from an AI perspective as well.
Okay, cool.
Well, those are all the questions I had.
We really ran through that very quickly.
Oh, wow.
Yeah.
Yeah, what questions do you have for me?
Yeah, I have.
So you talked a little bit, I have a few questions about kind of how things work.
You talked about, you want this, this person to be very high level metrics, how can you, how can we inform these teams to make sure the three month strategy is created?
How much of it is the, the growthx.ai platform doing the work that I might strategize or those account directors or account managers?
Or is it how much hands on is it?
I guess I'm wondering versus of of possibly How How
Well, so you're not, like, generating any content or executing on strategy.
Okay.

It's a lot of, like, you'll be leading measurement, and I think, like, we have, like, a mixed bag of some of our account strategists and account directors have, like, strong editorial backgrounds working at, like, agencies or journalism, and some of them have, like, more of SEO background.
So I think there's a little bit of a mix in terms of skill sets.

So it'll depend on kind of who you're working with and also, like, which clients you're working with on, like, how much you're leading the strategy versus supporting with, like, metrics or other things.
Our platform today doesn't have, like, a metrics dashboard.
We're building it.
So I think we will have one, like, probably in the next month.
Um, but you'll be leading...
And, like, the strategy creation and helping come up with, like, the plan of how we're going to execute.
And then the team will be executing all that.
Awesome.
I love that.
I'd be super excited to jump in.
And you said they have varying levels of expertise.
Some have just had, like, writing experience.
Some have had a little more experience.
Have you guys seen, and I don't know if you can answer this or not, but have you guys seen, like, have they done a lot of any SEO stuff where you're, okay, this person knows some.
Are you able to share any of that?
I mean, everybody has, like, some marketing agency or, like, in-house experience.
Like, and, I mean, we are, like, basically built on an SEO content strategy.
So, like, everybody's got the principles, very familiar with the principles.
Okay, that's awesome.
I've worked with, the reason I ask is I've worked
I've with editorial teams before, and I've managed a team of content writers across a software where I basically outsource the writing.
And it differed a lot based on how much each person knew.
And sometimes there was some coaching that I had to do with that person and say, kind of like, here's how headings work, here's how links work.
And I totally love doing that, too.
No, we're beyond that.
Okay, perfect.
Awesome.
I figured based on the growth of your clients, I just wanted to see and make sure.
So that's cool.
I'd really excited about that.
And what I want to know most kind of is, Andi, how do you, how do you like working at GrowthX?
I mean, you said it yourself, we're growing crazy fast.
So I think it's really just hanging on tight at this point.
But the, like, the potential of the platform and what we can do, and I think, like, the excitement around what we're doing is infectious.
So it's really fun to be part of that.
And...
Like the team is just like, we have a really scrappy team that's willing to jump in, do anything, help.
So I think like, it's a crazy time to be here and really fun, but you have to be willing to like, put it in the work and be scrappy to do it.
That's awesome.
Well, I'm definitely scrappy and I love putting it in the work.
So I'd be very excited.
Is there, is there anything else you can tell me about kind of how the team looks or how the daily operations look that we haven't covered already?
I'm just kind of curious.
Um, I mean, I think like, have you, you haven't talked to Jason yet, I'm guessing.
No.
Yeah.
I mean, like the, you'll kind of be split.
I, we haven't decided whether this role is going to report to me as like head of customer operations and like really thinking client facing or head of our, our head of growth.
Who's Jason.
Um, and he is, he manages some of our like biggest name clients, but.
We,
He's got, like, more of a coding background and is thinking more programmatically, like, writ large.
Like, he's driving GrowthX's growth engine as well.
And what he's really thinking deeply about right now is Geo, so, and experimenting with Geo, which is what I mentioned.

So I think, like, what's going to be really important in this role is being innately familiar with, like, a qualitative approach to strategy that is justifiable and defensible for every client, and that can, like, be applied to, like, content strategy.
Thank you so much.
That's really good information, and I love jumping into that, too, so I'd be super excited.
So thank you for jumping into what they need to do.
What do you think would be the highest, the best things that would make this person?
And the best fit for the role.
I mean, like, you have to be proactive.
And the ability to kind of, like, switch context very quickly.
Because you'll be working with so many clients.
And so, and then just, like, straight up organization.
Because we are looking for, like, signals that are, that we can talk about.
And tell clients, like, this is what you should do.
And this is why.
Like, we need documentation and organization.
We need, like, process around setting this strategy right now.
It's very ad hoc.
Like, how we're setting, how often and how we're setting strategy for different clients.
Everybody's got a bit of a different approach just based on their background.
And like I said, like, some clients were refreshing strategy constantly.
Because they have somebody that's thinking in that way.
But you'll have.

We to kind of be proactive and able to work like internally to convince teams and help teams like structure the messaging for strategy shifting and be organized enough to like kind of keep track of that across all clients so that we can kind of tell that story to other clients as we see results.
That absolutely makes sense.
I'm super organized and I love to build systems up that are repeatable and drive ROI.
Okay.

Also really curious, you talked a little bit about the team and I'm sorry if I'm repeating on this, but I wonder, are you able to describe if there are any PPC or like other marketing professionals in-house email or otherwise?
Sorry, what do you, like we're just doing like blog content strategy.
So we're not doing like paid, we're not, we're doing like, from a social perspective, we're only doing like LinkedIn content.
Strategy for thought leadership, and that's like on an ad hoc basis.
We're dabbling in newsletters, but I think there's like a lot that still needs to be done on the programmatic side there to make it repeatable.
But we're just looking at like organic blog content growth.
Okay, awesome.
And this position would be focused, it sounds like, purely on the client side, making sure that they're built out and not internally for growth.
Trying to build them up.
Yeah, I mean, I think like anything that we learn, we will apply, but Jason's, like Jason's taking the lead on that.
So I'm sure like there will be pieces that we will turn back internally, but this is really in service of our clients.
Okay, awesome.
Well, those are all the questions I had.
hope that it wasn't too much.
And I'm really passionate about this opportunity.
Would love to drive some ROI for the team and work with you and Jason more.
Yeah, awesome.
Okay.
Well, thank you, Brandon.
was great to chat with you.
Yeah.
Is there anything else that I can provide about my experience?
Actually, I guess like one thing that we didn't really talk about was your client-facing experience specifically.
I know you worked on like Embark was your client was like your employer.
So how do you think about like external facing work?
Absolutely.
So I worked for Embark and it was in internal, but they had more than 15 other different specific individually branded and marketed basically treatment centers.

So I was in charge of not only marketing for Embark Behavioral Health and their SEO, but also creating those marketing campaigns and SEO campaigns for these teams like Chrysalis, which is a therapeutic boarding school, residential centers across the U.S.
that were...
So I would market and create SEO strategies for more than 15 of them and jump into their marketing, jump into their domains, create the strategies for all of them.
So I'm really used to managing multiple things at once, multiple teams, and having to kind of pivot really quickly.
So that wouldn't be new to me.
I'm really passionate about it and love telling those other stories.
Perfect.
Great.
Well, thanks for giving that clarity.
It was helpful.
Thanks, Brandon.
I'll talk to you soon.
Thank you, Andi.
Have a good day.
It's good to talk to you.
Bye.
